reasoning: The resolution period is from 1 February 2026 to 31 March 2026. The current date is 15 February 2026. We know that North Korea conducted missile tests in January 2026 (Jan 4 and Jan 27), setting a high baseline tempo for the year. This period coincides with two major catalysts for North Korean missile testing. First, North Korea is scheduled to convene its 9th Congress of the Workers' Party in late February 2026. Historically, major party congruences are preceded or accompanied by significant military demonstrations, including missile launches. Second, the U.S. and South Korea are set to conduct their annual joint military exercise, Freedom Shield, from March 9 to 19, 2026. North Korea reliably objects to these joint drills, framing them as a "war rehearsal," and frequently responds by testing ballistic or cruise missiles (as it did during Freedom Shield exercises in 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025). The combination of a major domestic political event and a major joint US-ROK military exercise within this tight two-month window strongly incentivizes a provocation. There is slightly less certainty merely because they might delay for technical reasons or unexpected diplomatic shifts (which ISW noted that South Korea's MOU was advocating for postponing the exercise to preserve dialogue, though it appears scheduled). However, the base rate for North Korean missile testing during this window is already very high (approx. 85-90% over any 2-month period recently), and these specific events push the true probability slightly above baseline to around 92%, in line with the community's forecast.
